data.title: Keycorp targets 20% net interest income growth for 2025 amid strong loan and deposit momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KeyCorp (KEY) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Chris Gorman highlighted strong first-quarter results, including a 16% year-over-year revenue increase and flat expenses. Pre-provision net revenue rose over $90 million sequentially. Nonperforming assets declined nearly 10% quarter-over-quarter, reflecting improving credit quality.
  • Management emphasized the company's resiliency amid economic uncertainty, supported by a strong CET1 ratio of 11.8% and liquidity with over 30% of its balance sheet in cash and cash equivalents.
  • Strategic investments from last year, including a minority investment by Scotiabank, were noted as enhancing capital and earnings trajectories. Commercial loans increased by $1.2 billion sequentially, with elevated pipelines.
  • CFO Clark Khayat reported first-quarter earnings per share of $0.33. Tax-equivalent net interest income reached $1.1 billion, up 4% sequentially. Non-interest income increased 3% year-over-year, driven by growth in commercial mortgage servicing and payments.
  • Investment banking fees set a first-quarter record, with pipelines at historically elevated levels.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its full-year 2025 guidance, including 20% growth in net interest income and an anticipated 2.7% or better net interest margin by Q4 2025.
  • Non-interest income is expected to grow at least 5%, supported by mid-to-high single-digit growth in investment banking, wealth, and commercial payments.
  • Management noted tailwinds from structural factors like fixed-rate asset repricing and disciplined deposit beta management.
  • The environment remains dynamic, and flexibility was emphasized for adjusting share repurchases and other capital deployment strategies.

Financial Results

  • Revenue increased 16% year-over-year, driven by a 4% sequential rise in tax-equivalent net interest income.
  • Commercial mortgage servicing fees grew 36% year-over-year, reaching record levels. Investment banking fees were also a record for the first quarter.
  • Average loans were $105 billion on a period-end basis, with a notable increase in C&I loans offset by consumer loan runoff.
  • Deposits grew 4% year-over-year while interest-bearing deposit costs decreased by 18 basis points, contributing to a decline in overall funding costs.
  • The CET1 ratio stood at 11.8%, reflecting a strong capital position.

Q&A

  • Mike Mayo, Wells Fargo: Questioned how the company reconciles an uncertain economic environment with its confident guidance. CEO Gorman explained that client sentiment remains cautiously optimistic and the company has modeled various scenarios, including stagflation, to ensure preparedness.
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about C&I loan growth and utilization trends. CEO Gorman highlighted broad-based growth in regions like Southern California and Chicago, noting a recent uptick in utilization rates.
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, Bank of America: Inquired about CapEx project funding amid uncertainty. CEO Gorman noted that ongoing projects remain unaffected, while new projects may pause until more clarity emerges.
  • Nathan Stein, Deutsche Bank: Asked about market dislocation opportunities. CEO Gorman pointed to real estate transactions as an area where the company can gain share during volatile periods.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the macroeconomic environment but acknowledged the company's strong positioning and capital management strategies. Questions focused on reconciling the challenging backdrop with optimistic guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience and readiness for a range of economic outcomes. In the Q&A, the tone remained constructive, with detailed explanations of guidance assumptions and operational flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone of both analysts and management showed a slight increase in cautious optimism, reflecting improved credit metrics and ongoing strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent, reaffirming 20% net interest income growth for 2025. The previous quarter's guidance also highlighted strong momentum, but the current quarter placed greater emphasis on structural tailwinds and elevated pipelines.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from focusing on balance sheet repositioning last quarter to exploring growth opportunities and macroeconomic resilience this quarter.
  • Management's tone showed increased confidence in credit quality and investment banking, supported by record-setting performance metrics and improved asset quality indicators.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macroeconomic uncertainties, including inflation and geopolitical risks, which could impact client activity and market conditions.
  • Potential risks include prolonged pauses in transactional activity, further economic instability, and challenges in maintaining deposit growth and beta discipline.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the impact of tariffs and their potential second-order effects on client portfolios.

Final Takeaway

KeyCorp reported strong Q1 2025 results, driven by revenue growth, improving credit quality, and successful strategic investments. Management reaffirmed its guidance for 20% net interest income growth in 2025, supported by structural tailwinds and disciplined cost management. Despite economic uncertainties, the company emphasized its robust capital position and readiness to adapt to evolving market conditions, with a focus on supporting clients and leveraging investment opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Blackstone targets $1.2T AUM amid strong Q1 2025 growth in private credit and wealth channels
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Blackstone Inc. (BX) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Steve Schwarzman reported distributable earnings of $1.4 billion for Q1, up 11% year-over-year, highlighting it as one of the best quarters in the firm’s history. Assets under management (AUM) rose 10% year-over-year to a record $1.2 trillion, driven by $62 billion in inflows during the quarter.
  • Chairman Schwarzman emphasized the firm’s resilience amid market turbulence, crediting its structures that allow flexibility and long-term capital commitments. He mentioned $177 billion of dry powder available for capital deployment.
  • President Jon Gray highlighted the expansion of Blackstone’s private credit business, which grew to $465 billion in AUM, with $113 billion in inflows over the past year. He cited a 35% year-over-year growth in investment-grade private credit.
  • CFO Michael Chae noted fee-related earnings of $1.3 billion, representing a 9% year-over-year increase. He pointed to strong momentum in fee-related earnings and a record $1.9 billion in management fees during the quarter.
  • Blackstone announced a strategic alliance with Wellington and Vanguard to develop integrated public-private investment solutions, aiming for further expansion in private wealth channels.
  • BREIT (the Blackstone Real Estate Income Trust) achieved its best quarterly returns in 18 months, with a cumulative annualized net return of 9.4% since inception. Other perpetual products such as BXPE and BXINFRA also demonstrated strong growth.

Outlook

  • CEO Schwarzman expressed confidence in navigating economic uncertainty, emphasizing the firm’s ability to deploy capital during periods of market dislocation.
  • Blackstone plans to launch BMAX, a new diversified credit strategy for individual investors, on May 1, targeting additional growth in the private wealth segment. This product will offer daily subscriptions compared to monthly sales for other products.
  • The management reiterated its focus on areas benefiting from secular tailwinds such as digital infrastructure, energy, and private credit.
  • Despite market volatility, the firm expects continued inflows across private credit and wealth strategies.

Financial Results

  • Q1 distributable earnings reached $1.4 billion, or $1.09 per share. Fee-related earnings were $1.3 billion, and the firm declared a $0.93 dividend per share.
  • Net accrued performance revenue stood at $6.4 billion at the quarter-end.
  • Real estate core plus funds appreciated by 1.2%, while private equity funds achieved a 1.1% increase during the quarter. Non-investment grade credit strategies delivered quarterly gross returns of 2.7%.
  • Infrastructure strategies led performance with a 7.5% appreciation for the quarter, driven by digital infrastructure and energy-related investments.

Q&A

  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Questioned confidence in deploying $177 billion of dry powder. CFO Michael Chae explained that market dislocation offers opportunities in public equities, corporate partnerships, and assets in sectors like digital infrastructure and commercial real estate.
  • Brian McKenna, Citizens: Asked about the resilience of private markets. President Jon Gray highlighted the firm’s low leverage and long-term capital commitments as key advantages, allowing it to lean into opportunities during market downturns.
  • Craig Siegenthaler, Bank of America: Inquired about North American institutional fundraising amid DPI headwinds. Gray emphasized growing interest in credit and infrastructure, particularly in investment-grade private credit.
  • Alex Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Discussed the investment-grade credit opportunity. Gray pointed to tailored solutions like the $5 billion Rogers transaction as an example of Blackstone’s ability to deliver bespoke financing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed cautious optimism, commending Blackstone’s diverse growth engines but pressing for clarity on deployment strategies amid economic uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing the firm’s strategic positioning and resilience. In the Q&A, their responses were measured, addressing concerns about market volatility and capital deployment opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment from both management and analysts reflected increased confidence in fundraising and performance resilience across key strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • AUM growth accelerated from $1.1 trillion in Q4 2024 to $1.2 trillion in Q1 2025, driven by a higher $62 billion in inflows.
  • Fee-related earnings growth slowed slightly, reflecting a 9% year-over-year increase compared to the 76% growth seen in Q4.
  • Real estate fundraising showed improvements, with significant closings for European and credit funds, contrasting with the slower recovery pace highlighted in Q4.
  • Management’s tone was more optimistic about credit and private wealth channels, signaling stronger momentum compared to last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged potential second-order effects from tariffs, such as capital market volatility and economic slowdown risks. They also noted potential construction cost increases impacting real estate.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of flows into perpetual products like BREIT amid market uncertainty.
  • The firm acknowledged the importance of performance consistency to sustain confidence across its investor base.

Final Takeaway

Blackstone’s Q1 2025 results underscore its strong positioning with record AUM and robust growth in private credit and wealth channels. Management remains confident in navigating market volatility, emphasizing dry powder deployment and strategic initiatives like BMAX and the Wellington-Vanguard alliance. Investors will closely watch the firm’s ability to maintain strong inflows and performance across key strategies in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Santos says Pikka oil project in Alaska could start early; total Q1 production up, revenues down
data.description:
Santos (OTCPK:STOSF) +12.1% in early trading Thursday after saying it potentially will bring forward the startup for its Pikka phase 1 oil project in Alaska's North Slope, where first production currently is scheduled for mid-2026.

Santos (OTCPK:STOSF) said a 120-mile pipeline is now substantially complete, creating "the opportunity for an early startup, subject to weather and logistics, which will become clearer in the second quarter."

The company recently reached a deal with ConocoPhillips over the use of roads needed to access the Pikka field, ending a years-long dispute.

Pikka is expected to produce 80,000 bbl/day of oil at peak production in the initial phase, helping boost long-sagging production in Alaska.

Santos (OTCPK:STOSF) said it produced 21.9M boe in Q1, up slightly from 21.5M in Q4 2024, as volumes in Western Australia jumped by more than 18% Q/Q, underpinned by the Halyard-2 infill well.

Q1 revenue fell 8% Q/Q to $1.29B, missing a Visible Alpha consensus estimate of $1.32B, reflecting lower sales volumes and weaker prices for domestic gas and oil-linked liquefied natural gas sales contracts; the average realized price for the company's LNG fell to $11.57/MMBtu from last year's $12.68/MMBtu.

Santos (OTCPK:STOSF) said its Barossa natural gas development in Australia is now 95.2% complete and on track for first production in Q3, while Pikka's first phase is 82.2% complete.

data.title: Great Southern Bancorp GAAP EPS of $1.47
data.description:
• Great Southern Bancorp press release (NASDAQ:GSBC): Q1 GAAP EPS of $1.47.
  • Net interest income for the first quarter of 2025 increased $4.5 million (or approximately 10.1%) to $49.3 million compared to $44.8 million for the first quarter of 2024

data.title: Nanox gets FDA Clearance for new imaging system, Nanox.ARC X
data.description:
• Nano-X Imaging (NASDAQ:NNOX) said on Thursday that it received clearance from the U.S. FDA for the Nanox.ARC X, its digital tomosynthesis device.
  • The Nanox.ARC X system is used to produce tomographic images for general use, including musculoskeletal, pulmonary, intra-abdominal and paranasal indications.
  • Nanox will offer the Nanox.ARC X system, later this year, alongside the current Nanox.ARC, the company said.

data.title: Economic losses from catastrophes reach record highs in Q1 - report
data.description:
Insurance broker Aon (NYSE:AON) said on Thursday that global economic losses from catastrophes touched record highs in the first quarter, driven by California wildfires and other events, including storms in the U.S. and earthquakes in Myanmar and China.

The Q1 2025 economic losses amounted to at least $83B, higher than $54B in Q1 2024 and the 21st century Q1 average of $61B.

The "Q1 Global Catastrophe Recap" attributed the losses mainly to California wildfires and other billion-dollar events, including multiple storm outbreaks across the U.S. and earthquakes in Myanmar and China. 

U.S. economic losses alone accounted for ~$71B, highest since 1994 and above the $12B Q1 average since 2000. Meanwhile, the economic losses in all other regions were below their long-term Q1 averages.

First-quarter insured losses are expected at more than $53B, above the 21st century Q1 average of $17B. California wildfires contributed about $38B to the total, the report noted.

Earlier today, property and casualty insurer Allstate (ALL) announced estimated catastrophe losses of $1.04B for March, surging from $92M in February.

Related Stocks: Progressive (PGR), Chubb (CB), Tokio Marine Holdings (OTCPK:TKOMY), Travelers (TRV), American International Group (AIG), Fairfax Financial (OTCPK:FRFHF) (FFH:CA), Intact Financial (OTCPK:IFCZF) (IFC:CA), Hartford Insurance (HIG), Arch Capital (ACGL)

Related ETFs: SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (NYSEARCA:KIE), Invesco KBW Property & Casualty Insurance ETF (NASDAQ:KBWP), iShares U.S. Insurance ETF (NYSEARCA:IAK)

data.title: CVS Health to close over 20 stores in Arkansas after PBM law
data.description:
CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) said it will close more than 20 pharmacies in Arkansas after the state Gov. Sarah Huckabee Sanders signed a law banning pharmacy benefit managers ((PBMs)) from owning or operating pharmacies, marking the first of its kind legislation to pass in the U.S.

Named HB1150, the law comes as PBMs face intense regulatory scrutiny in the U.S. over their role in rising drug prices. In a recent report, the FTC said that PBMs owned by CVS Health (NYSE:CVS), Cigna (NYSE:CI), and UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) charged significant markups for many critically important generic drugs over six years. 

PBMs, also known as pharma middlemen, offer prescription drug coverage for big clients such as health insurers. In addition to CVS, CI, and UNH, which collectively administer about 80% of all prescriptions in the U.S., Elevance Health (NYSE:ELV) also operates in the PBM market.

“For far too long, drug middlemen called PBMs have taken advantage of lax regulations to abuse customers, inflate drug prices, and cut off access to critical medications. Not anymore,” Sanders said in a press release.

“Unfortunately, HB1150 is a bad policy that accomplishes just the opposite,” CVS (NYSE:CVS) said. The company, which operates 23 pharmacies and over 100 mail-order pharmacies in Arkansas, has been running TV commercials to scuttle the legislation and urging Sanders to veto it.

“It will take away access to pharmacy care in local communities, hike prescription drug spending across the state by millions of dollars each year, and cost hundreds of Arkansans their jobs,” CVS added.

data.title: Dow drops as UnitedHealth Group plunges, S&P, and Nasdaq also slide
data.description:
Major averages extended losses on Thursday, following a tech-led selloff the previous day, as UnitedHealth Group (UNH) plunged after cutting its full-year profit outlook in response to disappointing Q1 2025 results.

Early on and the S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.1%, the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.5%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.5%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 1 basis point to 4.29%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) dipped 1 basis point to 3.77%.

Wall Street on Wednesday ended deep in the red after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment.

"Ahead of the Easter weekend, risk assets sold off again, as ongoing trade tensions and hawkish comments from Fed Chair Powell led to growing concern about the near-term outlook, particularly if the Fed would be reluctant to ease policy," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"In terms of the latest on the trade front, investors were most concerned about chipmakers yesterday, which followed the new restrictions from the Donald Trump administration on semiconductor exports to China," Allen said.

On the economic front, the initial jobless claims unexpectedly dropped in the past week. 

Housing starts also dropped more than expected in March, and building permits surprised higher.

Markets will be closed tomorrow on account of Good Friday.

data.title: Trump Media asks SEC to look for stock manipulation after Qube's short position
data.description:
Trump Media and Technology (NASDAQ:DJT) sent a letter to the Securities and Exchange Commission on Thursday to look into "suspicious activity" involving the company's stock after Qube Research & Technologies disclosed a short position in the company.

On Monday, a Reuters report, citing a filing with Germany's federal gazette Bundesanzeiger, suggested that the UK-based hedge fund disclosed a nearly $105M short position in the Truth Social parent.

The memo to the SEC asked the regulator to investigate "potential illegal naked short selling and market manipulation of DJT stock."

The company has sent the same memo to the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority, Nasdaq, and the New York Stock Exchange.

Click to read the full memo sent by DJT to the SEC.

data.title: KeyCorp Q1 earnings beat on higher NII; credit loss provision jumps
data.description:
KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) stock drifted up 2% in Thursday morning trading after turning in Q1 earnings and revenue that rose past the average analyst estimate as the difference between the interest it earns from lending and the interest it pays on deposits climbed during the three-month period. Still, it said aside more capital to cover potential losses from loans at risk of default.

Meanwhile, the regional lender maintained its guidance for full-year 2025. 

Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.38 topping the $0.32 consensus, held steady from the prior quarter and rose from $0.22 a year before.

Total revenue (taxable equivalent) of $1.77B, vs. $1.75B expected, climbed from $865M in Q4 and $1.53B in Q1 2024.

Net interest income ("TE") advanced 4.1% sequentially and 24.7% Y/Y to $1.11B.

Noninterest income was $668M, up from -$196M in Q4 and $647M in the year-earlier quarter.

Trust and investment services income of fell to $139M from $142M in the previous quarter and edged up from $136M in last year's Q1.

Investment banking and debt placement fees were $175M compared with $221M in Q4 and $170M in Q1 2024.

Provision for credit losses jumped to $118M from $39M in Q4 and $101M in Q1 2024.

Average deposits inched up 0.9% Q/Q to $88.3B. Average loans of $104.4B vs. $104.7B in Q4.

Summing up Y/Y trends, Chairman and CEO Chris Gorman said: "Our first quarter results marked a strong beginning to the year. Revenue was up 16% year-over-year while expenses were essentially flat. We achieved both absolute and fee-based positive operating leverage on a year-over-year basis. Sequentially, net interest income grew 4% and the net interest margin increased by 17 basis points to 2.58%."
